stories.title: Singapore's MRT Circle Line Is Finally a Full Circle!
stories.headline_kid: Three brand-new MRT stations just opened, and now the Circle Line finally goes all the way around Singapore in one big loop!
summary: Imagine a hula hoop with one piece missing — that was Singapore's Circle Line for years. On 12 July 2026, three new stations called Keppel, Cantonment and Prince Edward Road finally opened, closing the gap and completing the loop after more than three decades of planning.

More than 10,000 commuters will now enjoy shorter, more direct train rides instead of having to change trains or take long bus routes. During peak hours, trains on the main loop will arrive roughly every 2 minutes, so there is much less waiting around on the platform.

Before the new stations officially opened, thousands of curious visitors and train enthusiasts turned up for a special preview, taking free rides and joining games at the stations. It shows how excited Singaporeans are about better ways to get around their island home.

At the same time, some other lines like the Downtown Line will have shorter operating hours on Friday nights until early September, while workers do important upgrading works. So if your family is planning a late outing, it's smart to check train timings first.

lenses.me: You might reach tuition, CCA or your friend's house faster if you live near one of the new stations, with less time spent waiting for trains.
lenses.family: Weekend trips to places like Sentosa or HarbourFront could become quicker and more convenient for the whole family.
lenses.friends: Classmates living around Keppel, Cantonment or Prince Edward Road can now meet up more easily to study or play together.
lenses.nation: Completing the Circle Line loop after 30 years shows how Singapore keeps planning ahead so our small island can move millions of people smoothly every day.
lenses.world: Singapore's train network is often studied by other cities as an example of careful, long-term transport planning.

compo_words.meaning: a shape that goes round and joins back to where it started
compo_words.example: The Circle Line finally forms a complete loop around the city.

compo_words.meaning: a person who travels regularly, especially to work or school
compo_words.example: Every morning, thousands of commuters squeeze onto the MRT.

compo_words.meaning: able to be trusted to work well
compo_words.example: A reliable train service means you rarely have to worry about being late.

compo_words.meaning: to show something to the public for the first time
compo_words.example: The three new stations were unveiled to excited crowds on 4 July.

compo_words.meaning: easy and useful, without causing trouble
compo_words.example: The new stations make travelling to Sentosa far more convenient.

compo_words.meaning: an important stage or event in the progress of something
compo_words.example: Completing the Circle Line loop is a major milestone for Singapore's transport system.

compo_phrases.meaning: to return to where something started after going through changes
compo_phrases.example: After 30 years of planning, the Circle Line has finally come full circle.

compo_phrases.meaning: to travel from one place to another
compo_phrases.example: The new stations help commuters get from A to B much faster.

compo_phrases.meaning: being planned or prepared for the future
compo_phrases.example: More MRT extensions are already in the pipeline for the next ten years.

questions: Have you ever ridden the MRT to a brand-new station? How did you feel exploring it for the first time?
Do you think having faster, more reliable trains makes people in Singapore happier? Why or why not?
If you could design a new MRT line for Singapore, where would it go and why?
phrases: This reminds me of a time when ...

sources.name: Malay Mail – New Circle Line set to transform Singapore's MRT commute
LTA – Explore Three New Circle Line Stage 6 Stations

stories.title: Get Ready: Hazy Skies May Return to Singapore
stories.headline_kid: Weather experts warn that hot, dry weather could bring the smoky haze back to Singapore skies between now and October.
summary: Weather scientists say Singapore is heading into hotter, drier months because of two weather patterns far out at sea called El Niño and the Indian Ocean Dipole. Think of them as the ocean's moods — when the Pacific and Indian Oceans warm up or cool down in certain spots, it changes winds and rain patterns all the way over here.

Drier weather means forests and peatlands in our neighbouring countries can catch fire more easily, often from land-clearing. When the wind blows the smoke our way, we get haze — a bit like smelling your neighbour's barbecue through the window, except the 'smoke' can cover the whole sky for days.

This is only the second time since 2019 that forecasters have issued a 'red alert' for haze risk, the first being back in 2023. In fact, fires in Johor already caused hazy weeks earlier this year, including one peatland fire that burned about 200 hectares of land.
To stay safe, families can check the PSI (Pollutant Standards Index) on the NEA's haze website, keep N95 masks at home, and be ready for schools to move sports or recess indoors on very hazy days.

lenses.me: You might need to keep a mask in your school bag and expect PE or recess to move indoors if the haze gets bad.
lenses.family: Your family may need to close windows, switch on an air purifier, and check on grandparents or family members with breathing problems.
lenses.friends: Outdoor CCA training or sports days might be postponed, so keep an eye on school announcements with your classmates.
lenses.nation: Singapore's environment agency works with neighbouring countries to try to reduce forest fires that cause transboundary haze.
lenses.world: It shows how connected our planet is — a fire started in one country's forest can affect the air that millions of people breathe in another.

compo_words.meaning: crossing over borders between countries
compo_words.example: Transboundary haze happens when smoke from one country drifts into another.

compo_words.meaning: an official warning or piece of advice
compo_words.example: NEA issued a haze advisory asking people to limit outdoor activities.

compo_words.meaning: to predict what will happen, especially with weather
compo_words.example: Scientists forecast hotter, drier weather for Singapore this year.

compo_words.meaning: an action taken in advance to prevent something harmful
compo_words.example: Keeping a mask ready at home is a sensible precaution against haze.

compo_words.meaning: a substance that makes the air, water or soil dirty or unsafe
compo_words.example: Smoke from forest fires releases pollutants into the air we breathe.

compo_words.meaning: to watch and check something carefully over time
compo_words.example: Weather stations monitor air quality every hour during the haze season.

compo_phrases.example: Parents should keep an eye on the PSI readings during haze season.

compo_phrases.meaning: to act cautiously to avoid danger
compo_phrases.example: The school decided to play it safe and cancel the outdoor carnival.

compo_phrases.meaning: to make a situation better or less tense (here also literally true!)
compo_phrases.example: Everyone hoped the rain would come soon to clear the air of haze.

questions: Do you remember a time when the sky in Singapore looked hazy? How did the air feel?
Why do you think it's important for countries to work together to stop forest fires?
What would you do if your teacher suddenly cancelled outdoor sports because of haze?
phrases: I think it is important because ...
One thing we can do is ...

sources.name: The Star – Increased risk of haze in Singapore from June to October due to El Niño, Indian Ocean Dipole
Human Resources Online – El Niño to bring hotter and drier weather to Singapore, increasing risk of haze
Inquirer – High risk of severe haze in Singapore, SE Asia in 2nd half of 2026

stories.title: A Teochew Movie Has Singapore Talking About Dialects
stories.headline_kid: A Singapore movie made in the Teochew dialect has become so loved that more cinemas are showing it, and now the government is rethinking old rules about dialects.
summary: Long before most children spoke Mandarin or English at home, many grandparents in Singapore grew up speaking dialects like Hokkien, Teochew and Cantonese. Decades ago, Singapore encouraged everyone to speak Mandarin instead of dialects to make it easier for the Chinese community to understand one another, so dialects were rarely heard on TV or in cinemas.

Now, a Teochew-language film called Dear You has touched the hearts of many Singaporeans, especially seniors who grew up speaking the dialect. Because so many people wanted to watch it, Singapore's media regulator, IMDA, approved 100 more screenings of the film.

This has sparked bigger conversations about whether Singapore's rules on dialects in films and shows should change. Officials have said any changes will be gradual and carefully targeted, not one big, sudden rewrite of the rules.

For children today, this story is a gentle reminder that dialects carry family memories and history. Chatting with grandparents in their dialect, even just a few words, can be a wonderful way to feel closer to them.

lenses.me: You could learn a few fun Teochew, Hokkien or Cantonese words from your grandparents and feel proud of your family's roots.
lenses.family: Watching a dialect film together could become a special bonding activity with grandparents or elderly relatives.
lenses.friends: You and your classmates from different dialect groups could share interesting words or phrases during a class sharing session.
lenses.nation: Singapore is trying to balance keeping our many cultures and heritage alive while staying united as one nation.
lenses.world: Many countries around the world are also working hard to keep old languages and dialects from disappearing.

compo_words.meaning: traditions, language and culture passed down through generations
compo_words.example: Learning a dialect is one way to connect with your family's heritage.

compo_words.meaning: a way of speaking a language that is special to a certain region or group
compo_words.example: My grandmother speaks the Teochew dialect at home.

compo_words.meaning: to keep something safe from being lost or damaged
compo_words.example: The film helps preserve the sound and spirit of the Teochew dialect.

compo_words.meaning: true and genuine, not copied or fake
compo_words.example: The movie gives an authentic look at life in an older Singapore.

compo_words.meaning: a group of people born and living around the same time
compo_words.example: The older generation grew up speaking dialects rather than Mandarin.

compo_words.meaning: an official rule that controls how something is done
compo_words.example: IMDA is reviewing its regulation on dialects in local films.

compo_phrases.meaning: something that feels personal or familiar
compo_phrases.example: The story in the film felt close to home for many elderly viewers.

compo_phrases.meaning: to stay connected to one's family background and culture
compo_phrases.example: Learning dialect words helps children stay in touch with their roots.

compo_phrases.meaning: to show something clearly without needing many words
compo_phrases.example: The full cinema halls speak volumes about how much people love the film.

questions: Do your grandparents speak a dialect? What words or phrases do you know?
Why do you think it is important to keep old languages and dialects alive?
If you could learn one dialect, which would you choose, and why?
phrases: I think this is important because ...

sources.name: Google News Singapore – dialect policy and Dear You coverage

stories.title: World Cup Fever! Four Great Teams Reach the Semifinals
stories.headline_kid: France, Spain, England and Argentina have all reached the semifinals of the 2026 World Cup — the excitement is building!
summary: The FIFA World Cup is the biggest football competition on Earth, played once every four years. This year's tournament, held across the United States, Canada and Mexico, features more teams than ever before.
After weeks of thrilling matches, four strong teams have made it through to the semifinals: France, Spain, England and Argentina. Only two of these teams will go on to play in the grand final.

A semifinal match is one of the last four games before the championship — winning it means a team is just one step away from lifting the World Cup trophy. Football fans everywhere, including many here in Singapore, are gathering at home, in coffee shops and at public screenings to cheer on their favourite teams.

Even though Singapore does not have a team in this World Cup, the tournament is a great time to enjoy football together with family and friends, and to see how sport can bring people from completely different countries together in excitement.

lenses.me: You get to enjoy exciting matches with family, and maybe learn a bit more about geography and different countries.
lenses.family: Match nights can turn into fun family gatherings, complete with snacks and cheering.
lenses.friends: You and your classmates can chat about which team you think will win and support your favourites together.
lenses.nation: Big sporting events like this can inspire more Singaporean children to take up football and other sports.
lenses.world: The World Cup shows how a single sport can unite millions of people worldwide, no matter their language or background.

compo_words.meaning: a series of matches or games to decide an overall winner
compo_words.example: The World Cup is the most-watched football tournament in the world.

compo_words.meaning: a match played to decide who will compete in the final
compo_words.example: Argentina fought hard to win their semifinal match.

compo_words.meaning: a person who watches an event without taking part
compo_words.example: Thousands of spectators filled the stadium to watch the match.

compo_words.meaning: a strong will to keep trying and not give up
compo_words.example: The players showed great determination in the final minutes of the game.

compo_words.meaning: a great victory or achievement
compo_words.example: Reaching the semifinals was a triumph for the whole team.

compo_words.meaning: a person or team competing against another
compo_words.example: France and their rivals battled fiercely for a place in the final.

compo_phrases.phrase: on the edge of one's seat
compo_phrases.meaning: feeling very excited or nervous while watching something
compo_phrases.example: Fans were on the edge of their seats during the last five minutes.

compo_phrases.meaning: to try as hard as possible
compo_phrases.example: Every player gave it their all to reach the semifinals.

compo_phrases.meaning: very close in a competition, with no clear leader
compo_phrases.example: The two teams were neck and neck until the final whistle.

questions: Do you have a favourite football team or player? What do you like about them?
What lessons can we learn from watching team sports like football?
If Singapore had a team in the World Cup, how do you think our country would celebrate?
phrases: What I admire about this team/player is ...

sources.name: Yahoo News Singapore – World Cup semifinals coverage
Google News Singapore – World Cup coverage

stories.title: Trouble Near a Faraway Sea Could Affect Prices Here
stories.headline_kid: Fighting between countries near the Gulf has put an important sea route at risk, and that could make fuel more expensive, even in Singapore.
summary: There is a serious and worrying conflict happening far away, involving Israel, the United States and Iran, with fighting reported near the Gulf region. It is a difficult situation, and grown-ups around the world, including in Singapore, are watching it closely and hoping for peace.

One reason this matters so much is a narrow sea passage called the Strait of Hormuz. Picture it like a doorway that ships must pass through — a huge amount of the world's oil travels through this doorway on its way to countries everywhere.

Singapore does not have its own oil, so we buy fuel from other countries. We are also one of the world's busiest ports and a major hub for trading and refining oil, which means any trouble near the Strait of Hormuz can push up oil prices around the globe, including here.

If tensions continue, families might notice petrol, bus fares or even the price of goods shipped by sea creeping up slightly. It is a good reminder that news happening on the other side of the world can still reach right into our homes.

lenses.me: You might hear grown-ups at home talking about rising prices and wonder why — now you know it can be linked to events far away.
lenses.family: Your family might decide to drive less or save more energy at home if fuel becomes more expensive.
lenses.friends: This is an interesting topic to discuss in class about how connected our world really is.
lenses.nation: Singapore's government and companies keep a close watch on global energy news because our economy relies heavily on trade and shipping.
lenses.world: It shows how a conflict in one region can create ripple effects on economies and daily life across the entire planet.

compo_words.meaning: a serious disagreement or fight between groups or countries
compo_words.example: The conflict in the Gulf region has worried many countries.

compo_words.meaning: a path taken to get from one place to another
compo_words.example: The Strait of Hormuz is an important shipping route for oil.

compo_words.meaning: the system of trade and money in a country or region
compo_words.example: Singapore's economy depends a lot on international trade.

compo_words.meaning: extremely important or absolutely necessary
compo_words.example: Oil is an essential resource for transport and industry.

compo_words.meaning: a strong effect or influence on something
compo_words.example: Rising oil prices can have an impact on everyday bus and taxi fares.

compo_words.meaning: relating to the whole world
compo_words.example: Oil prices are decided by global supply and demand.

compo_phrases.meaning: to watch something closely because it may be important
compo_phrases.example: Singapore's leaders are keeping an eye on the situation in the Gulf.

compo_phrases.meaning: a situation where one event causes a series of other effects
compo_phrases.example: The tension in the Gulf could have a ripple effect on prices worldwide.

compo_phrases.meaning: to have a cost or downside, even if not obvious at first
compo_phrases.example: Cheaper oil in the past came at a price of relying on unstable regions.

questions: Have you noticed grown-ups at home talking about rising prices recently? How did that make you feel?
Why do you think events happening far away can still affect life here in Singapore?
What are some ways your family could save fuel or energy at home?
phrases: One way we can help is by ...
This is important to me because ...
